name: PR Controller
description: Scheduled triage of open GitHub PRs. Fans out across PRs, classifies each by diff signature, routes to a specialist reviewer (database-optimizer, security-engineer, performance-benchmarker, or accessibility-auditor), aggregates findings into a dated triage report. Headless and cron-safe.

data.name: Phase Settings
data.phase_goal: Triage open PRs by risk and route reviews to the right specialist persona.
data.success_criteria:
Open PRs fetched from GitHub
Each PR classified by diff signature
Each PR reviewed by the matching specialist persona
Dated triage report written to pr-triage/

data.name: Classify by diff signature
data.routing_prompt:
Classify the PR (title, description, and changed files) into exactly one route based on what it primarily touches.

Routes:
- Route 1: DB / migrations â€” schema changes, ALTER TABLE, new migrations, index changes, ORM model edits affecting persisted data.
- Route 2: Auth / crypto / security â€” authentication, authorization, token handling, password/credential code, encryption, signing, CORS, CSRF, session logic, anything in auth/security modules.
- Route 3: Performance-sensitive paths â€” hot loops, query builders, caching, batching, concurrency primitives, allocation-heavy code, render paths, code clearly on a critical performance path.
- Route 4: Frontend / accessibility â€” UI components, templates, CSS, ARIA, keyboard handlers, color/contrast, anything user-facing without backend impact.

If multiple routes apply, pick the one with the highest blast radius in the order: 1 > 2 > 3 > 4.

Respond with ONLY the route number (1, 2, 3, or 4). Nothing else.

data.name: Migration safety review
data.prompt:
You are reviewing a GitHub PR that primarily touches database schema, migrations, or persisted data. Do NOT modify any files. Produce a focused written review only.

Read the PR title, description, and diff from context. Evaluate:
- Lock contention risk: will this migration take exclusive locks on hot tables? Is it online-safe under load?
- Irreversible operations: column drops, type narrowings, destructive backfills, missing rollback path.
- Backfill plan: is there a backfill? Is it batched? Idempotent? Resumable on failure?
- Index strategy: new indexes created concurrently? Unused indexes left behind? Cardinality assumptions sound?
- Data integrity: NOT NULL added without default, FK changes, constraint timing, replication impact.
- Migration ordering: safe deploy order (expand-then-contract) vs breaking the running app.

Output a markdown review with these sections:
## PR Reference
(PR number, title, author)
## Risk Level
P0 (block) | P1 (must fix before merge) | P2 (follow-up) | P3 (nit)
## Findings
Numbered list of concrete issues, each citing the relevant file/line where possible.
## Recommended Actions
Concrete next steps for the author.

Keep it under 400 words. Be specific, no generic advice.

data.name: Security review
data.prompt:
You are reviewing a GitHub PR that primarily touches authentication, authorization, cryptography, or security-sensitive code. Do NOT modify any files. Produce a focused written review only.

Read the PR title, description, and diff from context. Evaluate:
- Token handling: storage, transport, rotation, scope, expiry. Anything ending up in logs, URLs, or client storage?
- Injection surfaces: SQL, command, template, header, deserialization. Are inputs validated/escaped at the trust boundary?
- Authentication changes: password hashing parameters, MFA logic, session fixation, cookie flags (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite).
- Authorization changes: new endpoints behind the right guard? IDOR risk? Privilege boundaries respected?
- Crypto: algorithm choice, key length, IV/nonce reuse, constant-time comparisons, randomness source.
- Secrets: hardcoded credentials, config leakage, dependency CVEs in added packages.

Output a markdown review with these sections:
## PR Reference
(PR number, title, author)
## Risk Level
P0 (block) | P1 (must fix before merge) | P2 (follow-up) | P3 (nit)
## Findings
Numbered list of concrete issues, each citing the relevant file/line where possible. Map each to a category (AuthN / AuthZ / Crypto / Injection / Secrets / Dependencies).
## Recommended Actions
Concrete next steps for the author.

Keep it under 400 words. No generic OWASP boilerplate â€” only what's actually in this diff.

data.name: Performance review
data.prompt:
You are reviewing a GitHub PR that primarily touches performance-sensitive code paths. Do NOT modify any files. Produce a focused written review only.

Read the PR title, description, and diff from context. Evaluate:
- Hot paths: is this code on a per-request, per-frame, or per-tick path? Did the change increase work inside the hot loop?
- Allocation: new allocations per call, boxing, defensive copies, unnecessary string concatenation, reused buffers vs fresh objects.
- Algorithmic complexity: any O(n^2) lurking where O(n) was acceptable? Loops over loops over network/disk?
- Query plans: new ORM queries â€” N+1 risk, missing indexes, full scans, ORDER BY on unindexed columns, LIMIT pushdown.
- Concurrency: lock granularity, contention, blocking calls inside async, executor sizing.
- Caching / batching: opportunity missed or invalidation broken?

Output a markdown review with these sections:
## PR Reference
(PR number, title, author)
## Risk Level
P0 (block) | P1 (must fix before merge) | P2 (follow-up) | P3 (nit)
## Findings
Numbered list of concrete issues with file/line citations. Each finding states the expected impact (latency, throughput, allocations, query count).
## Recommended Actions
Concrete next steps, including any benchmark or profile the author should run before merge.

Keep it under 400 words. Cite numbers where you can; otherwise say so.

data.name: A11y review
data.prompt:
You are reviewing a GitHub PR that primarily touches frontend / user-facing code. Do NOT modify any files. Produce a focused written review only.

Read the PR title, description, and diff from context. Evaluate:
- ARIA: semantic HTML preferred over ARIA; when ARIA is used, are roles, states, and properties correct? aria-label / aria-labelledby on icon-only controls?
- Keyboard navigation: focus order, focus trap in modals, visible focus ring not removed, Esc/Enter handling, tabindex misuse.
- Color and contrast: text vs background ratio (>=4.5:1 for body, 3:1 for large), color used as the only signal (state, errors).
- Screen reader: alt text on meaningful images, empty alt on decorative, live regions for async updates, form labels associated to inputs.
- Motion / timing: respect prefers-reduced-motion, no time-limited interactions without override.
- Responsive / zoom: layout holds at 200% zoom, no horizontal scroll, target sizes meet 24x24 (or 44x44 mobile).

Output a markdown review with these sections:
## PR Reference
(PR number, title, author)
## Risk Level
P0 (block) | P1 (must fix before merge) | P2 (follow-up) | P3 (nit)
## Findings
Numbered list of concrete issues with file/line citations. Map each to a WCAG 2.2 success criterion where applicable.
## Recommended Actions
Concrete next steps for the author.

Keep it under 400 words. Skip generic a11y advice not grounded in this diff.
